title: The Writer's Dream
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A writer wrote of the hearts of men, and he followed their tracks afar;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For his was a spirit that forced his pen to write of the things that are.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His heart grew tired of the truths he told, for his life was hard and grim;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His land seemed barren, its people cold, yet the world was dear to him;,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So he sailed away from the Streets of Strife, he travelled by land and sea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In search of a people who lived a life as life in the world should be.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he reached a spot where the scene was fair, with forest and field and wood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And all things came with the seasons there, and each of its kind was good;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There were mountain-rivers and peaks of snow, there were lights of green and gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And echoing caves in the cliffs below, where a world-wide ocean rolled.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lives of men from the wear of Change and the strife of the world were free,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Steam was barred by the mountain-range and the rocks of the Open Sea.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the last that were born of a noble race, when the page of the South was fair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The last of the conquered dwelt in peace with the last of the victors there.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He saw their hearts with the authorן¿½s eyes who had written their ancient lore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he saw their lives as heן¿½d dreamed of such, ah! many a year before.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½Iן¿½ll write a book of these simple folk ere I to the world return,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And the cold who read shall be kind for these, and the wise who read shall learn.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Never again in a song of mine shall a jarring note be heard:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Never again shall a page or line be marred by a bitter word;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But love and laughter and kindly hours will the book Iן¿½ll write recall,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With chastening tears for the loss of one, and sighs for their sorrows all.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Old eyes will light with a kindly smile, and the young eyes dance with glee,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And the heart of the cynic will rest awhile for my simple folk and me.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lines ran on as he dipped his pen, ran true to his heart and ear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like the brighter pages of memory when every line is clear.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The pictures came and the pictures passed, like days of love and light,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He saw his chapters from first to last, and he thought it grand to write.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the writer kissed his girlish wife, and he kissed her twice for pride:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis a book of love, though a book of life! and a book youן¿½ll read!ן¿½ he cried.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was blind at first to each senseless slight (for shabby and poor he came)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From local ן¿½Fashionן¿½ and mortgaged pride that scarce could sign its name.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What dreamer would dream of such paltry pride in a scene so fresh and fair?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the local spirit intensified, with its pitiful shams, was there;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There were cliques wherever two houses stood (no rest for a family ghost!)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They hated each other as women could, but they hated the stranger most.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The writer wrote by day and night and he cried in the face of Fate,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll cleave to my dream of life in spite of the cynical ghosts that wait.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis the shyness born of their simple lives,ן¿½ he said to the paltry pride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (The homely tongues of the simple wives neן¿½er erred on the generous side),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Theyן¿½ll prove me true and theyן¿½ll prove me kind ere the year of grace be passed,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But the ignorant whisper of ן¿½axe to grind!ן¿½ went home to his heart at last.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The writer sat by his drift-wood fire three nights of the South-east gale,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ His pen lay idle on pages vain, for his book was a fairy tale.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The world-wise lines of an elder age were plain on his aching brow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he sadly thought of each brighter page that would never be written now.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll write no more!ן¿½ But he bowed his head, for his heart was in Dreamland yet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The pages written Iן¿½ll burn,ן¿½ he said, ן¿½and the pages thought forget.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he heard the hymn of the Open Sea, and the old fierce anger burned,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he wrenched his heart from its dreamland free as the fire of his youth returned:,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The weak manן¿½s madness, the strong manן¿½s scorn, the rebellious hate of youth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½From a deeper love of the world are born! And the cynical ghost is Truth!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the writer rose with a strength anew wherein Doubt could have no part;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll write my book and it shall be true, the truth of a writerן¿½s heart.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ay! cover the wrong with a fairy tale, who never knew want or care,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A bright green scum on a stagnant pool that will reek the longer there.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½You may starve the writer and buy the pen, you may drive it with want and fear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But the lines run false in the hearts of men, and false to the writerן¿½s ear.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The bardן¿½s a rebel and strife his part, and heן¿½ll burst from his bonds anew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Till all pens write from a single heart! And so may the dream come true.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Tis ever the same in the paths of men where money and dress are all,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The crawler will bully wheneן¿½er he can, and the bully who canן¿½t will crawl.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And this is the creed in the local hole, where the souls of the selfish rule;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Borrow and cheat while the strangerן¿½s green, then sneer at the simple fool.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Spit your spite at the men whom Fate has placed in the head-race first,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And hate till death, with a senseless hate, the man you have injured worst!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½There are generous hearts in the grinding street, but the Hearts of the World go west;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For the men who toil in the dust and heat of the barren lands are best!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The strangerן¿½s hand to the stranger, yet, for a roving folk are mine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The strangerן¿½s store for the stranger set, and the camp-fire glow the sign!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The generous hearts of the world, we find, thrive best on the barren sod,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And the selfish thrive where Natureן¿½s kind (theyן¿½d bully or crawl to God!)

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I was born to write of the things that are! and the strength was given to me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I was born to strike at the things that mar the world as the world should be!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By the dumb heart-hunger and dreams of youth, by the hungry tracks Iן¿½ve trod,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll fight as a man for the sake of truth, nor pose as a martyred god.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By the heart of ן¿½Billן¿½ and the heart of ן¿½Jim,ן¿½ and the men that their hearts deem ן¿½white,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½By the handgrips fierce, and the hard eyes dim with forbidden tears!, Iן¿½ll write!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Iן¿½ll write untroubled by cultured fools, or the dense that fume and fret,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For against the wisdom of all their schools I would stake mine instinct yet!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For the cynical strain in the writerן¿½s song is the world, not he, to blame,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And Iן¿½ll write as I think, in the knowledge strong that thousands think the same;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And the men who fight in the Dry Country grim battles by day, by night,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Will believe in me, and will stand by me, and will say to the world, ן¿½Heן¿½s right!ן¿½ן¿½
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
